---
title: "vSLAM"
subtitle: "An interactive guide to <i>Visual Simultaneous Location And Mapping</i>"
author: "Theodor Peifer, Amelie Hora, Japheth Legesse<br/>Munich University of Applies Sciences"
jupyter: python3
bibliography: references.bib
csl: ieee.csl
---

## Abstract
Visual SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) is a computer vision technique that allows a camera to estimate its motion while simultaneously building a 3D map of an unknown environment. It works by detecting and tracking visual features across images, estimating camera movement between frames and reconstructing scene structure from multiple views. vSLAM is widely used in robotics and autonomous navigation and relies only on a camera instead of depth sensors.

## Introduction
There are many different algorithms and approaches yet the general pipeline looks like the following:

```{=html}
<div class="demo-frame">
<iframe src="pages/html/vslam_pipeline.html" width="100%" height="1010" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" loading="lazy" onload="this.nextElementSibling.style.display='none'"></iframe>
<div class="demo-loading"><div class="demo-spinner"></div>loading…</div>
</div>
```

Run frame after frame, these steps are called **visual odometry** [@nister2004vo]: the camera is tracked while the map is built up as it goes. We focus on each step in turn, with interactive demos using OpenCV running directly in your browser.

## Pipeline

### 1. Feature Detection

{{< include pages/01-feature-detection.qmd >}}

---

### 2. Feature Matching

{{< include pages/02-feature-matching.qmd >}}

---

### 3. Camera Motion Estimation

{{< include pages/03-motion.qmd >}}

---

### 4. Triangulation

{{< include pages/04-triangulation.qmd >}}

---

### 5. 3D Point Cloud Map

{{< include pages/05-pointcloud.qmd >}}

---

### BONUS - Loop Closure

{{< include pages/06-loop-closure.qmd >}}

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
